text: The laparoscopic view shows adhesions of the gastrocolic omentum attached to the body of the gallbladder. The surgeon carefully dissects these adhesions to expose the bottom of the gallbladder, ensuring the duodenum is safely positioned away from the dissection area.

The surgeon begins dissection at the bottom of the gallbladder, opening the peritoneum over the anticipated location of the cystic duct near the ursulcus. The peritoneum is opened on the patient's right side first, enhancing retraction and manipulation of the gallbladder, especially for gallbladders with a short mesentery or intrahepatic position. The surgeon then proceeds to open the peritoneum on the left or medial side, facilitating better retraction for dissecting the cystic artery and duct to achieve the critical view.

The surgeon creates a window behind the gallbladder, releasing the artery to drop back into the hepatocystic triangle. The cystic duct and artery are clearly visible, achieving the critical view of safety. The surgeon meticulously cleans the triangle to ensure no hidden structures are present, and proceeds to safely clip the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the liver bed, utilizing the previously dissected lateral peritoneum for assistance. The dissection reveals a clear plane between the gallbladder and liver bed, facilitating the separation. An instrument is placed behind the gallbladder to provide a surface for cauterization as the dissection reaches the top of the gallbladder.

The surgeon positions the camera at the subxiphoid port to facilitate the introduction of a specimen retrieval bag through the paraumbilical port. The gallbladder, now separated, is placed into the retrieval bag for removal.
